verbs.actions.wash: When you wash, you clean something with water and soap.
You rub and scrub until it is clean, then rinse.
You wash your hands, your face, or the dishes.
verbs.actions.sit: When you sit, you rest your bottom on a chair or the floor.
You bend your knees and lower yourself down.
You sit down at the table or to read a book.
verbs.actions.stand: When you stand, you are up on your two feet.
You straighten your legs and hold yourself tall.
You stand up to walk or to reach something high.
verbs.actions.walk: When you walk, you move by stepping one foot at a time.
You step left, then right, and keep going.
You walk to get from one place to another.
verbs.actions.catch: When you catch, you grab something moving toward you.
You hold out your hands and close them around it.
You catch a ball when a friend throws it.
verbs.actions.wave: When you wave, you move your hand to say hi or bye.
You open your hand and swing it side to side.
You wave hello when you see someone.
verbs.actions.hug: When you hug, you wrap your arms around someone.
You squeeze them gently and feel close.
You give a hug to show you care.
verbs.actions.more.think: When you think, you use your brain to figure something out.
Ideas pop into your head when you think.
You think hard to solve a puzzle or make a choice.
verbs.actions.more.know: When you know something, you have it in your memory.
It means you learned it and you are sure.
You can say 'I know!' when you have the answer.
verbs.actions.more.remember: When you remember, you think of something from before.
It comes back into your mind.
You might remember a fun day or where you put a toy.
verbs.actions.more.see: When you see, you look at something with your eyes.
Your eyes take in the colors and shapes around you.
You can say 'I see it!' when you spot something.
verbs.actions.more.hear: When you hear, you take in a sound with your ears.
Sounds reach your ears, like music or a voice.
You hear a dog bark or someone call your name.
verbs.actions.more.feel: When you feel something, you sense it with your body or your heart.
You feel soft and hard with your hands, and happy or sad inside.
You can say 'I feel happy' or 'it feels soft.'
verbs.actions.more.try: When you try, you give something your best effort.
You keep going even when it is a little hard.
You can say 'I'll try!' before something new.
verbs.actions.more.make: When you make something, you put it together so it exists.
You use your hands and ideas to build or create it.
You can make a drawing, a tower, or a snack.
verbs.actions.more.do: When you do something, you make it happen.
You take action and get it done.
You can say 'I did it!' when you finish.
verbs.actions.more.get: When you get something, you go and bring it to you.
You reach for it or fetch it.
You can say 'I'll get it' to grab something.
verbs.actions.more.put: When you put something somewhere, you set it down there.
You move it and place it in a spot.
You put your toys in the bin and your cup on the table.
verbs.actions.more.take: When you take something, you pick it up and bring it with you.
You grab it and hold onto it.
You can take a toy or take a turn.
verbs.actions.more.carry: When you carry something, you hold it and move it.
You lift it in your arms or hands and walk.
You carry your backpack or carry a toy.
verbs.actions.more.share: When you share, you let someone else have some too.
You give a part or take turns with it.
You share your snack or a toy with a friend.
verbs.actions.more.say: When you say something, you use words out loud.
You make sounds and words with your mouth.
You can say 'hi' or say what you want.
verbs.actions.more.tell: When you tell, you share words with someone.
You give them news or let them know something.
You can tell a grown-up if something is wrong.
verbs.actions.more.ask: When you ask, you say a question to find out something.
You use words to ask for help or to know more.
You can ask 'what is that?' or ask for a turn.
verbs.actions.more.answer: When you answer, you reply to a question.
You say back what someone asked you.
You answer 'yes' or 'no' when someone asks.
verbs.actions.more.talk: When you talk, you use words with someone.
You take turns saying things back and forth.
You talk with your family and friends.
verbs.actions.more.watch: When you watch, you keep your eyes on something.
You look carefully as it moves or happens.
You watch a show or watch a bug crawl.
verbs.actions.more.clean: When you clean, you make something neat and tidy.
You wipe, scrub, or put things away.
You clean up your toys after you play.
verbs.actions.more.drive: When you drive, you steer a car to go places.
You sit and turn the wheel to make it go.
A grown-up drives the car to the store.
verbs.actions.more.fix: When you fix something, you make it work again.
You put the broken part back together.
You can say 'fix it, please' if something breaks.
verbs.actions.extra.kiss: When you kiss, you press your lips to show love.
You make a little 'mwah' with your lips.
You give a kiss goodnight to your family.
verbs.actions.extra.hop: When you hop, you jump on one foot or like a bunny.
You push off and land in a little bounce.
You hop on one foot or hop like a frog.
verbs.actions.extra.skip: When you skip, you step and hop along as you go.
You step, hop, step, hop, in a happy bounce.
You skip down the path when you feel cheerful.
verbs.actions.extra.swim: When you swim, you move yourself through the water.
You kick your legs and paddle your arms.
You swim in a pool or a lake.
verbs.actions.extra.blow: When you blow, you push air out of your mouth.
You pucker your lips and puff out air.
You blow bubbles or blow out birthday candles.
verbs.actions.extra.paint: When you paint, you make art with a brush and paint.
You dip the brush and sweep colors on paper.
You paint a picture with bright colors.
verbs.actions.extra.dig: When you dig, you scoop dirt to make a hole.
You use a shovel or your hands to scoop.
You dig in the sand or the garden.
verbs.actions.extra.splash: When you splash, you smack the water and it flies up.
You hit the water and droplets spray everywhere.
You splash in the bath or a puddle.
verbs.actions.extra.color: When you color, you fill a picture with crayons.
You move the crayon to fill in the shapes.
You color inside the lines of a coloring book.
verbs.actions.extra.cut: When you cut, you use scissors to slice through paper.
You open and close the scissors along a line — they are sharp, so careful.
You cut paper shapes for a craft.
verbs.actions.extra.glue: When you glue, you stick things together.
You dab on sticky glue and press the pieces down.
You glue paper onto a craft.
verbs.actions.extra.stir: When you stir, you mix something around in circles.
You hold a spoon and swirl it round and round.
You stir the batter or stir your soup.
verbs.actions.extra.ride: When you ride, you sit on something that moves you along.
You hold on and go as it rolls or moves.
You ride a bike, a swing, or in the car.
verbs.actions.extra.fly: When you fly, you move through the air.
You glide and soar up in the sky like a bird.
Birds and planes fly high above you.
verbs.actions.extra.fall: When you fall, you tip over and go down.
You lose your balance and drop to the ground.
You can say 'I fell!' if you tumble down.
verbs.actions.extra.fix: When you fix something, you make it work again.
You put the broken part back together.
You can say 'fix it, please' if something breaks.
verbs.actions.expr.laugh: When you laugh, you make happy 'ha-ha' sounds.
Your tummy shakes and a big smile spreads.
You laugh when something is funny.
verbs.actions.expr.cry: When you cry, tears come out because you feel sad or hurt.
Your eyes get watery and you might sniffle.
You can cry when you need a hug or feel owie.
verbs.actions.expr.smile: When you smile, you turn your mouth up to look happy.
Your lips curve and your cheeks lift.
You smile when you feel glad or see a friend.
verbs.actions.expr.frown: When you frown, you turn your mouth down because you feel upset.
Your eyebrows scrunch and your lips dip.
You might frown when something is not fair.
verbs.actions.expr.shout: When you shout, you use a big, loud voice.
You open your mouth wide and let the sound out loud.
You might shout to call someone far away.
verbs.actions.expr.whisper: When you whisper, you talk in a tiny, quiet voice.
You speak so soft it is almost a secret.
You whisper when you need to be very quiet.
verbs.actions.expr.squeeze: When you squeeze, you press something tight in your hands.
You wrap your fingers around it and give a gentle press.
You squeeze a toy or squeeze for a hug.
verbs.actions.expr.pinch: When you pinch, you press something between two fingers.
You bring your thumb and finger together on it.
You pinch a tiny bit of something, gently.
verbs.actions.expr.bounce: When you bounce, you spring up and down.
You push down and pop back up, again and again.
You bounce a ball or bounce on the bed.
verbs.actions.expr.roll: When you roll, you turn over and over along the ground.
You tumble in a circle, round and round.
You roll a ball or roll down a grassy hill.
needs.describe.dirty: 'Dirty' means covered in dirt or mess. The opposite is clean.
Muddy boots, sticky hands, and a messy face are dirty.
You can say 'I'm dirty!' or 'my hands are dirty.'
needs.describe.clean: 'Clean' means not dirty — all fresh and tidy. The opposite is dirty.
Washed hands, a wiped table, and clean clothes are clean.
You can say 'all clean!' after you wash up.
needs.describe.weather.sunny: 'Sunny' means the sun is out and bright.
A sunny day is warm with a clear blue sky.
You can say 'it's sunny — let's go outside!'
needs.describe.weather.cloudy: 'Cloudy' means the sky is full of gray clouds.
On a cloudy day, you can't see the sun much.
needs.describe.weather.rainy: On a rainy day, water falls and the ground gets wet.
You wear boots when it's rainy.
needs.describe.weather.snowy: 'Snowy' means snow is falling and covering the ground.
On a snowy day, everything turns white and cold.
You bundle up when it's snowy.
needs.describe.weather.windy: 'Windy' means the air is blowing strong.
On a windy day, leaves swirl and your hair blows.
It's a great day to fly a kite when it's windy.
needs.describe.weather.warm: 'Warm' means a little hot, but nice and cozy. The opposite is cool.
A sweater, a hug, and a sunny day feel warm.
You can say 'I feel warm and cozy.'
needs.describe.weather.cool: 'Cool' means a little cold, but fresh and nice. The opposite is warm.
A breeze, the shade, and cool water feel cool.
You can say 'this is nice and cool.'
needs.describe.more.funny: A silly joke, a clown, or a goofy face is funny.
You can say 'that's funny!' when you giggle.
needs.describe.more.new: 'New' means just made or just gotten. The opposite is old.
A new toy, new shoes, or a new baby are new.
You can say 'I got something new!'
needs.describe.more.pretty: A flower, a sunset, and a sparkly dress are pretty.
You can say 'so pretty!' when you like how something looks.
needs.describe.weather.more.stormy: 'Stormy' means there is a big storm with wind and rain.
On a stormy day, you might hear thunder and see lightning.
You stay cozy inside when it's stormy.
needs.describe.weather.more.foggy: 'Foggy' means the air is full of low, misty clouds.
On a foggy morning, it is hard to see far.
Everything looks soft and gray when it's foggy.
needs.describe.weather.more.lightning: 'Lightning' is a bright flash in the sky during a storm.
It zigzags and lights up the dark clouds.
Lightning is followed by a big boom of thunder.
needs.describe.weather.more.tornado: A tornado is a spinning funnel of strong wind.
It twists down from the sky and whirls very fast.
A tornado is powerful, so you stay safe inside.
needs.describe.weather.more.rainbow: A rainbow is an arc of colors in the sky.
It has red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and purple.
You might see a rainbow after the rain.
needs.describe.core.on: 'On' means turned on, or resting on top of something. The opposite is off.
The light is on, the TV is on, the cup is on the table.
needs.describe.core.off: 'Off' means turned off, or not on something. The opposite is on.
The light is off, the TV is off, your shoes are off.
You can say 'turn it off' or 'take it off.'
needs.describe.core.easy: 'Easy' means simple to do — not hard.
Putting on socks can be easy; a big puzzle is harder.
You can say 'that's easy!' when something is simple.
needs.describe.core.heavy: 'Heavy' means it weighs a lot and is hard to lift. The opposite is light.
A big rock, a full bag, and a fat book are heavy.
You can say 'this is too heavy!'
needs.describe.core.light: 'Light' means it weighs very little and is easy to lift. The opposite is heavy.
A feather, a balloon, and a leaf are light.
needs.describe.opp.old: 'Old' means it has been around a long time. The opposite is new.
An old toy, old shoes, and grandparents are old.
You can say 'this one is old.'
needs.describe.opp.young: 'Young' means very new in age — just little. The opposite is old.
A baby, a puppy, and a tiny seedling are young.
A young kid is just a few years old.
needs.describe.opp.closed: 'Closed' means shut, not open. The opposite is open.
A closed door, a closed box, and closed eyes are shut.
needs.describe.opp.shiny: 'Shiny' means it glows and reflects the light. The opposite is dull.
A new coin, a clean spoon, and a star are shiny.
needs.describe.opp.dull: 'Dull' means not shiny — flat and plain. The opposite is shiny.
Old metal, a cloudy day, and dusty things look dull.
needs.describe.opp.thick: 'Thick' means wide or chunky, not thin. The opposite is thin.
A big blanket, a fat book, and thick soup are thick.
You can say 'that's a thick one!'
needs.describe.opp.thin: 'Thin' means skinny or narrow, not thick. The opposite is thick.
A piece of paper, a noodle, and a twig are thin.
needs.describe.opp.deep: 'Deep' means it goes down very far. The opposite is shallow.
The deep end of a pool and the deep sea go way down.
needs.describe.opp.shallow: 'Shallow' means not deep — only a little down. The opposite is deep.
A puddle and the shallow end of a pool are shallow.
It's safe to stand in shallow water.
needs.describe.opp.rough: 'Rough' means bumpy and scratchy to touch. The opposite is smooth.
Sandpaper, tree bark, and a rocky path are rough.
needs.describe.opp.smooth: 'Smooth' means flat and soft to touch — no bumps. The opposite is rough.
Glass, a baby's cheek, and a calm lake are smooth.
needs.describe.opp.strong: 'Strong' means having lots of power. The opposite is weak.
A weightlifter, an ox, and a big crane are strong.
needs.describe.opp.weak: 'Weak' means not having much strength. The opposite is strong.
You might feel weak when you are very tired or sick.
A weak push barely moves anything.
needs.describe.opp.correct: 'Right' means correct — the answer that matches. The opposite is wrong.
Two plus two is four — that's right!
You can say 'that's right!' when something is correct.
needs.describe.opp.wrong: 'Wrong' means not right — a mistake. The opposite is right.
Putting a shoe on the wrong foot feels wrong.
It's okay to be wrong; you just try again.
needs.describe.opp.early: 'Early' means before the usual time. The opposite is late.
Waking up before the sun is early.
needs.describe.opp.late: 'Late' means after the time you were supposed to. The opposite is early.
Going to bed past bedtime is late.
needs.describe.opp.first: 'First' means before all the others — number one. The opposite is last.
The first in line gets to go before everyone.
needs.describe.opp.last: 'Last' means after all the others — at the very end. The opposite is first.
The last in line goes after everyone else.
The last piece is the final one.
needs.describe.opp.there: 'There' means in that spot, away from you. The opposite is here.
You point and say 'put it there.'
needs.describe.opp.less: 'Less' means not as much. The opposite is more.
A little bit of juice is less than a full cup.
needs.describe.opp.equal: 'Equal' means the same amount — they match.
Two cookies each is equal, fair and even.
When it's equal, no one has more than the other.
needs.describe.opp.longer: 'Longer' means more long than another. The opposite is shorter.
A snake is longer than a worm; a bus is longer than a car.
You can say 'this one is longer.'
needs.describe.opp.shorter: 'Shorter' means less long than another. The opposite is longer.
Your little finger is shorter than your arm.
needs.describe.opp.asleep: 'Asleep' means sleeping with your eyes closed. The opposite is awake.
A napping baby and a sleeping puppy are asleep.
You are asleep at night in your bed.
needs.describe.opp.awake: 'Awake' means not sleeping — eyes open. The opposite is asleep.
In the morning you wake up and are awake.
needs.describe.opp.noisy: 'Noisy' means full of loud sounds. The opposite is quiet.
A busy street, a party, and banging pots are noisy.
needs.describe.opp.safe: 'Safe' means not in danger — okay and protected.
A car seat, a helmet, and a grown-up's hand keep you safe.
needs.describe.opp.everywhere: When toys are everywhere, they are all over the room.
needs.describe.opp.nowhere: 'Nowhere' means in no place at all.
When something is nowhere, you can't find it anywhere.
You might say 'it's nowhere!' when a toy is lost.
needs.describe.character.kind: 'Kind' means nice and caring to others.
Sharing, helping, and gentle words are kind.
You can be kind by helping a friend.
needs.describe.character.mean: 'Mean' means unkind or hurtful. The opposite is kind.
Pushing or saying mean words is not nice.
It's better to be kind than mean.
needs.describe.character.gentle: 'Gentle' means soft and careful, not rough.
You pet a kitty gently and hug a baby gently.
needs.describe.character.friendly: 'Friendly' means warm and nice to be around.
A friendly person smiles, waves, and says hi.
You are friendly when you welcome someone.
needs.describe.character.helpful: 'Helpful' means you help and make things easier.
Cleaning up and carrying things are helpful.
You can say 'I want to be helpful!'
needs.describe.character.polite: You are polite when you take turns nicely.
needs.describe.character.careful: 'Careful' means paying attention so you stay safe.
You are careful near a hot stove or sharp things.
needs.describe.character.busy: When you're busy, you are working hard at something.
needs.describe.character.smart: 'Smart' means good at learning and thinking.
You are smart when you solve a puzzle or learn a word.
needs.describe.character.lazy: 'Lazy' means not wanting to move or do much.
On a lazy day, you just rest and relax.
A lazy cat naps all afternoon.
needs.describe.character.fancy: 'Fancy' means extra special and dressed up.
A sparkly dress and a decorated cake are fancy.
You wear fancy clothes on a special day.
needs.describe.character.silly_more: Making funny faces and goofy dances is goofy.
